mnemonics.title: DFA vs NFA Memory Aid
mnemonics.content:
D-Determinism: 'Definitely One Transition' - DFA has exactly one next state for each input
N-Non-determinism: 'N umerous Next states possible' - NFA can have multiple or zero next states
Both accept Regular Languages - 'Both Accept Really' (BAR)

mnemonics.title: Steps to Convert NFA to DFA
mnemonics.content:
E-CLOSE: Find ε-closure
CREATE: Create new DFA states as subsets
TRANSITION: Find transitions for each input
CHECK: Mark accepting if subset has accepting state
REPEAT: Continue until all states processed
Mnemonic: 'ECTR' (Eat, Create, Transition, Repeat)

mnemonics.title: Regular Expression Operators
mnemonics.content:
Star (*) = Zero or more (Kleene Star)
Plus (+) = One or more (Union + concat)
Question (?) = Zero or one
Pipe (|) = OR (Union)
Mnemonic: 'S P Q P' - Star is zero or more, Plus is one or more, Question is optional, Pipe is or

acronyms.usage: General term for both DFA and NFA in Theory of Computation
Automaton with exactly one transition per input symbol from each state
Automaton allowing multiple or zero transitions per input symbol
Maps Q × Σ → Q in DFA
Maps Q × Σ → P(Q) in NFA, power set of states
Algebraic notation to represent regular languages
Represents zero-length string in transitions and expressions

visualTips.visualization: Draw circles for states, arrows for transitions labeled with input symbols. Start state has incoming arrow from nowhere. Accepting states have double circle.
Each DFA state is a bubble containing the subset of NFA states it represents. If any NFA accepting state is in the subset, mark DFA state as accepting.
Build small NFAs for each symbol, then combine using three templates: Union (two paths), Concatenation (series), Star (loop back)
Use table-filling method. Put (p,q) in table if one is accepting and other is not. Mark pairs. Unmarked pairs are equivalent and can be merged.

analogies.concept: Finite Automaton as a Board Game
analogies.analogy: Think of a finite automaton like a board game piece. The game board has finite squares (states). You roll dice (input symbols) and move to different squares according to rules (transition function). You win (accept) if you land on a winning square (accepting state) after all dice are rolled.

A regular expression is like a recipe. 'a*b' means take any number of 'a' ingredients (zero or more), then one 'b'. '*' means you can repeat that ingredient any number of times, just like following a recipe repeatedly.

analogies.concept: DFA vs NFA as GPS Routes
analogies.analogy: DFA is like a GPS with only one recommended route for each destination - deterministic. NFA is like a GPS showing all possible routes - you can take any one. Both will get you to the same places (accept the same language) if you follow the rules.

ε-transitions are like teleportation - you move between states without consuming any input (without traveling any 'distance'). ε-closure is finding all places you could teleport to from your current location, including staying where you are.
